text: The surgeon begins the procedure on the right side by dissecting the tumor from the undersurface of the liver up to the diaphragm. The robotic instruments' endo-wrist movements aid in creating a plane between the inferior vena cava and the tumor. The adrenal vein is identified, dissected, clipped with hemolog clips, and divided. The surgeon carefully dissects the tumor's undersurface and from the upper pole of the kidney, clipping and dividing major vascular attachments to prevent bleeding. Clips and bipolar energy are used to manage the vascular nature of the pheochromocytoma, while preserving as much normal adrenal tissue as possible.

The surgeon preserves as much normal adrenal tissue as possible. The excised specimen is packed and placed under the left lobe of the liver. Hemostasis is achieved using Surgicel for low-pressure, low-volume bleeding.

The robotic instruments are repositioned for surgery on the left side. The surgeon carefully dissects surrounding structures, including the spleen, tail of the pancreas, upper pole of the kidney, and colon, away from the left adrenal gland. The adrenal vein is looped and controlled to preserve it during the adrenal-sparing procedure. The surgeon performs enucleoresection of the tumor from the lateral limb of the left adrenal gland using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, with hemolog clips applied as needed. Some bleeding is observed during the dissection process.
